plainEnglish: Every UI component must expose its name (what it's called), role (what kind of thing it is—button, checkbox, tab), and value/state (checked, expanded, selected) to assistive tech, and notify them when these change. Native HTML elements do this for free; custom widgets built from <div>s need the right ARIA roles, properties, and states.
whyItMatters: Screen reader users can't understand or operate a custom control built from <div>s—they don't hear what it is, what it's called, or whether it's on or off—unless its name, role, and state are exposed.
quickCheck: Inspect custom widgets in the Chrome DevTools Accessibility pane and confirm each exposes a name, a correct role, and current state; axe flags missing names (button-name, link-name, label) and invalid ARIA.
commonMistakes: Building buttons, checkboxes, or tabs from <div>s with no role and no ARIA state — use native elements or add role plus aria-*.
Buttons, links, or inputs with no accessible name (axe button-name, link-name, label).
A custom toggle that never updates aria-checked or aria-expanded when its state changes.
Invalid or unsupported ARIA attributes and roles (axe aria-allowed-attr, aria-roles).
An icon-only button whose only content is an SVG with no label.

codeExamples.label: Native button exposes name, role, and state
codeExamples.code:
{/* aria-pressed exposes the toggle state; the role and name are built in */}
<button aria-pressed={on} onClick={toggle}>Mute</button>

codeExamples.label: Div toggle exposes nothing to assistive tech
codeExamples.code:
{/* No role, no name, no state */}
<div className="toggle" onClick={toggle} />
